original_prompt: Please reschedule my Dental check-up to 2024-05-20 at 10:00.
assessment_reasoning: The user specifies the event name and the desired new date/time, so the primary goal is clear. However several execution details are not specified: which matching calendar event to pick if there are multiple, the event end time or duration (so the agent must infer/keep the original duration), timezone handling, and whether/what kind of notification (email) should be sent after rescheduling — the verification expects an outgoing email even though the user didn’t mention it. The ground-truth logic requires searching for the event and then rescheduling by event_id and updating both start and end times, plus creating an inbox email; these steps are not explicitly requested by the user. Overall the prompt is neither extremely vague nor fully explicit—it leaves some important operational details open.

variations.L2: Please reschedule my Dental check-up to May 20, 2024, sometime in the morning (around 10:00 AM).
variations.L3: Please reschedule my Dental check-up to 2024-05-20 at 10:00.
variations.L4: Find the calendar event titled "Dental check-up" and reschedule it to start on 2024-05-20 at 10:00 AM. Keep the event's original duration the same and notify both me and the clinic by email about the new time.
variations.L5: Do the following exactly: 1) Search my calendar for the event with title "Dental check-up" (use search_calendar_events with query='Dental check-up') and get its event_id and original start/end times. 2) Compute the original duration (original_end - original_start). 3) Call reschedule_calendar_event with arguments event_id=<the id>, new_start_time='2024-05-20 10:00' (format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM), and new_end_time equal to new_start_time plus the original duration (e.g., '2024-05-20 10:30' if the original duration was 30 minutes). 4) After rescheduling, add a confirmation email to my inbox notifying the clinic and me of the new appointment time. Only update that event and preserve all other calendar items.
